deductions.reason: The tutor consistently kept the work student-led, but never clearly explained why those constraints support learning, retention, or independent analytical skill-building.

After the student improved from a weaker claim or vague reasoning to a more defensible formulation, the tutor affirmed the improvement and moved on without prompting reflection on what changed and why the revision worked better.

grade.overview: Across the bundle, the tutor was consistently strong at guided discovery, assignment anchoring, and concise interactive questioning. The main weakness was meta-learning: the tutor did not explicitly explain why the student-led constraints were pedagogically useful, and it missed chances to prompt reflection after clear improvement. No major ambiguity affected scoring, so deductions were limited to those two well-supported rubric points.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor avoided giving submission-ready answers and instead used structured prompts, comparisons, and evidence tests to keep idea generation student-led. It also stayed tightly anchored to the assignment, redirected toward concrete actor categories, and built effectively on student progress without noticeable spiraling or off-topic drift. I deducted under 1.3.A because the tutor never explicitly stated the learning rationale behind its constraint-based approach, even when saying things like it would critique logic rather than write for the student. I also deducted under 1.3.C because, after students revised claims into stronger versions, the tutor validated the improvement but did not ask them to reflect on what changed in their reasoning.
